required.title: [Book] Alexander, Who Used to Be Rich Last Sunday
required.author: Judith Viorst

required.title: [Supplies] A clear jar, envelope, or three containers

suggested.title: [Book] The Berenstain Bears' Trouble with Money
suggested.author: Stan and Jan Berenstain

suggested.title: [Activity] Grocery store trip with a budget

competence_markers.description: The student can explain that money comes from work and does not appear from nowhere
The student can identify whether common items are needs or wants
The student can compare relative costs — understanding that some things cost much more than others
The student uses the Save / Spend / Give system consistently when receiving money
The student has chosen a saving goal and is tracking progress toward it
The student can make spending decisions with a limited budget and explain their reasoning
The student can identify at least three major household expenses and explain that someone must earn money to pay them
The student has earned money through personal effort at least once

cross_connections.description: Money is applied math. Counting coins, adding prices, calculating change, tracking savings — all of this is arithmetic in action.

Saving requires delayed gratification — the same self-control muscle developed in IC1.1. Name this connection: "You are practicing discipline every time you put money in the Save jar instead of spending it."
Food costs money. The grocery trip in this packet connects directly to the food awareness developed in 2.3.1. Food budgeting begins here.
Consistent saving, consistent allocation, consistent tracking — these are discipline practices applied to money.

content.why_this_matters:
Money is one of the most important things in the world that you cannot see working. You see people hand over cards and tap phones at the store, and things appear in bags. It looks like magic. It is not.

Behind every purchase is real money — and behind every dollar is real work. Someone worked hours, days, or weeks to earn that money. When your family buys groceries, fills the car with gas, or pays for the house you live in, they are spending money that someone earned by doing something valuable for other people. Money does not come from machines or apps. It comes from work.

Here is something else that is important: money runs out. There is not an endless supply. Your family has a certain amount of money, and they have to decide how to use it. Some of that money pays for things you absolutely need — food, a home, electricity, water. Some of it pays for things you want but do not need — toys, treats, entertainment. The difference between needs and wants is one of the most important things you will ever learn about money, because understanding that difference is what keeps people from running out of money when they need it most.

You are going to start learning how money works — not the complicated adult version, but the real foundation: money comes from work, things cost different amounts, spending is a choice, and saving is a skill.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding that money represents work** — the connection between labor, value, and earning
2. **Awareness of relative cost** — things cost different amounts; some things cost a lot more than others
3. **The needs vs. wants distinction** — needs must be met first; wants come after and within limits
4. **Basic saving skills** — the discipline of setting money aside rather than spending it immediately
5. **Experience with spending decisions** — making choices about how to use a limited amount of money
6. **Early awareness that a household has costs** — the home, food, electricity, and water all cost money that someone must earn
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Where Does Money Come From?

Money comes from work. When an adult goes to a job, they do something that other people value — they build things, fix things, teach things, drive things, help people, make things, sell things. In exchange, they receive money. That money is how they pay for everything your family needs.

Some people work in offices. Some people work in hospitals. Some people work in stores, on farms, in schools, in trucks, in restaurants, on construction sites. Every job that earns money is doing something that someone else is willing to pay for.

Here is the important idea: before money arrives in your family, someone worked for it. When you ask for a toy at the store, you are asking for something that required hours of someone's work to afford. That does not mean you should never ask — it means you should understand what you are asking for. Money is not free. It is traded for time and effort.

**What about allowance?** If your family gives you an allowance — a small amount of money each week — that money also came from someone's work. Your allowance is your family sharing some of what they earned so you can begin learning how money works. Treat it with respect.

**What about money you earn?** If you do extra chores, help a neighbor, or run a small project (a lemonade stand, a craft sale), you earn money directly. You trade your time and effort for money. This is the most direct way to understand what money really is.

### Part 2: Things Cost Different Amounts

Not everything costs the same amount. Some things cost very little. Some things cost a lot. Understanding relative cost helps you understand the world.

Here are some rough examples (prices vary, but the relationships are real):

- A loaf of bread: about $3-4
- A gallon of milk: about $4-5
- A new bicycle: about $100-300
- A month of electricity for a house: about $100-200
- A month of rent or mortgage for a house: about $1,000-3,000 (or more, depending on where you live)
- A used car: about $5,000-15,000
- A new car: about $30,000-50,000

Look at those numbers. A loaf of bread costs about $4. A month of living in a house might cost $2,000. That means the cost of your home for one month is the same as five hundred loaves of bread. Your home is expensive. It costs a lot of money every single month, and that money has to be there or your family cannot stay.

**Why this matters:** When you understand that big things (a home, a car, electricity, food for a whole family) cost a lot — and they come first — you begin to understand why your family sometimes says "not right now" to things you want. It is not because they do not care about what you want. It is because the big needs have to be paid first, and there is a limited amount of money to work with.

### Part 3: Needs vs. Wants

This is one of the most important ideas about money:

**Needs** are things you must have to live safely and healthily. Food, water, shelter (a home), clothing, heat in winter, medical care. Without these, you are in danger.

**Wants** are things you would like to have but could live without. Toys, candy, video games, new shoes when your current ones still fit, eating at a restaurant instead of cooking at home. These are not bad things. They make life more enjoyable. But they are not needs.

The rule is simple: **needs come first.** You pay for the house, the food, the electricity, and the water before you pay for anything else. After the needs are covered, and after you have saved some money (more on that in a moment), what is left can go toward wants.

Many adults get this wrong. They spend money on wants — expensive phones, new clothes they do not need, eating out every day — before making sure their needs and savings are fully covered. This is how people end up in financial trouble: not because they do not earn enough, but because they spend in the wrong order.

You are learning the right order now.

### Part 4: Saving — The Skill That Changes Everything

Saving means keeping some money instead of spending it. This sounds simple. It is one of the hardest financial skills for most people to develop, because spending feels good right now and saving only pays off later.

**Your saving system:** You need three containers — jars, envelopes, or boxes — labeled:

- **SAVE** — Money you are setting aside for a bigger goal. You do not touch this money until you reach your goal.
- **SPEND** — Money you can use now for things you want (after needs are covered, which at your age, your family handles).
- **GIVE** — Money you set aside to give to others — a charity, a friend in need, an offering at your place of worship.

Every time you receive money — allowance, earnings from work, gifts — divide it into these three categories. A common starting split is: 50% Save, 40% Spend, 10% Give. Your family may choose different proportions. The important thing is that saving happens first, not with whatever is left over.

**The saving experience:** Choose something you want that costs more than you currently have. Write it down. Write down the price. Every time you put money in your Save jar, count the total. Watch the total grow. This is slow, and that is the point. The discipline of watching money accumulate — of not raiding the jar when you want something else — is the same discipline that adults need to build an emergency fund, save for a home, or prepare for retirement. You are building the muscle now.

### Part 5: Making Spending Decisions

When you have money to spend, you face choices. You cannot buy everything you want with a limited amount of money, so you have to decide.

Here is a practice exercise: Imagine you have $10 to spend at a store. You want three things:
- A candy bar ($2)
- A small toy ($7)
- A pack of stickers ($4)

Total cost: $13. You have $10. You cannot buy all three. What do you do?

You could buy the candy bar and the toy ($9 total, $1 left). You could buy the candy bar and the stickers ($6 total, $4 left). You could buy just the toy ($7, $3 left). You could buy just the stickers ($4, $6 left to save).

There is no single right answer. But there is a process: look at what you want, look at what you have, and decide what matters most to you. Then accept the decision without regret. This is how adults manage money too — just with bigger numbers.

### Part 6: Your Family Spends Money to Run Your Home

Your family pays for things every month to keep your home running and your family fed, clothed, and safe. These are some of the regular costs:

- **Housing** — rent or a mortgage payment (the biggest cost for most families)
- **Food** — groceries for the whole family, every week
- **Utilities** — electricity, gas, water, sewer, trash collection, internet
- **Transportation** — car payments, gas, insurance, maintenance, or bus/train passes
- **Insurance** — health insurance, car insurance, home insurance
- **Clothing** — replacing worn-out clothes and shoes, seasonal needs

These costs add up to thousands of dollars every month. Somebody earns that money through their work, and somebody manages it — decides how much goes where, pays the bills on time, and makes sure there is enough.

You do not need to manage this yet. But you should know it is happening. The house you live in, the food you eat, the heat that keeps you warm — none of it is free. All of it costs money. All of it requires someone's work and someone's careful management.

Understanding this is the beginning of financial awareness. It is also the beginning of gratitude — not the kind you are told to feel, but the kind that comes from understanding what it actually takes to run the home you live in.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Money-Work Connection
**Developing well:** The child can explain, in their own words, that money comes from work. They can describe what adults they know do to earn money. They understand that the things they receive cost money that someone worked for.

**Developing slowly:** The child still treats money as something that comes from a card or a machine. They have no connection between labor and income.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done Activity 1 (interviewing adults about their work)? Have you discussed, in concrete terms, what your work involves and how it produces the income that supports the household? Children need to hear real stories from real people, not abstract principles.

### 2. Needs vs. Wants Distinction
**Developing well:** The child can categorize common items as needs or wants without prompting. When shopping, they spontaneously identify wants and accept the prioritization of needs. They do not argue that a want is a need.

**Developing slowly:** The child treats everything they desire as a need. They cannot articulate the difference, or they articulate it but do not apply it to their own desires.

**Diagnostic questions:** Practice the distinction in low-stakes settings. At the grocery store: "Is this a need or a want?" At home: "Let's look around — what are the things in this room that are needs? What are wants?" Repetition in context is what builds the framework.

### 3. Saving Behavior
**Developing well:** The child consistently allocates money to Save, Spend, and Give when they receive it. They can state their saving goal and current progress. They resist the urge to spend Save money on impulse purchases.

**Developing slowly:** The system exists but is not being used consistently. The child "forgets" to allocate, or the Save jar never grows because money keeps migrating to Spend.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the allocation happening every time money is received, or only sometimes? Is the saving goal posted somewhere visible? Is the progress visible (a chart on the wall, a count on the jar)? Visibility reinforces discipline for young children.

### 4. Spending Decision Quality
**Developing well:** When given a limited budget, the child considers options, compares prices, and makes a deliberate choice. Afterward, they can explain why they chose what they chose and accept what they gave up.

**Developing slowly:** The child grabs the first thing they see, or agonizes without being able to decide, or is upset afterward about what they did not get.

**Diagnostic questions:** How many times has the child practiced making real spending decisions with real (limited) money? This is a skill that develops through repetition. One trip to the store is not enough. The $10 Decision activity should be repeated multiple times.
